sentences.text: [BACKGROUND NOISES] [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

Right, when we came to Siemiatycze, sort-- the situation there-- people were going and coming, and, well, my parents were so very happy that we got out of Germany and that, hopefully, that will be so, for us, the end of the war.
But there was a lot of confusion because a lot of people were going back.

We met quite a few people-- well, my father's friends-- like that would come and say that, oh, they'd left some family in Warsaw or somewhere else, and the situation in-- in the Russia occupied zone is not so hot, and they're going back.
You know, and we-- so anyway, we were confused because we thought that this is where we are going to stay, and very hopefully, and here, we saw people, young people, going back.

Unfortunately, later on, history proved them very wrong because thousands of Jews would survive if they would not [INAUDIBLE] they wouldn't go back, if they would stay on, and so-- but-- but for us, it was a bit of confusion.
Anyway, so we stayed in Siemiatycze for almost a week, or perhaps a bit more, and then my father decided-- or probably he had it in plan already before-- that we are going to Lutsk.
Lutsk was a very big city.
In a-- and my father hoped to be able to get maybe some kind-- he had some friends there that he knew from before.
He thought that perhaps he'll be able to get a job, and we came to Lutsk.
That family that we came to was a very lovely family.
They were very nice people.
They were quite wealthy people.
They helped us to establish.
For us, the situation wasn't very bright because my father didn't get a job, and there was a lot that were called [NON-ENGLISH].
They were-- this is a Russian word for people that were on the run, so they run away from Germany.
And we were-- we were also one of them, of course, and we-- we were thinking that what-- I mean, my father were hoping that he'll get a job.
See that he couldn't get a job, he was a bit upset, but he was still hopeful.
I-- I remember he got sick.
You couldn't get medication.
We're completely no medication.
The situation with food was also not great.
I remember my father had a coat, which was called [NON-ENGLISH].
This is the type of coats that they have.
It's a fur coat, a very warm coat.
It was a very bitter-- it was a bitter cold winter, and for everything, there was a queue.
You know, the Russians brought already their system, and Lutsk was already very much so like the Russian system.
For everything, he was standing in a queue, and I remember my brother and myself, the whole night, whoever was standing in the queue would get that coat.
And we would start like 6:00 in the evening and would stand until 9 o'clock in the morning, until the shops will open.
Sometimes, you would get some salt, which you could change later on.
Sometimes, you would get-- and sometimes you would spend the whole night and get really nothing.
I remember, we were standing, and then they were selling men's ties, which we-- we didn't need.
So this was-- we were it Lutsk like this, sitting there, waiting for a job.
My father got sick.
As I said, we couldn't get the medication, but still, we were quite happy that we got away from the Germans.
Then one day, in the middle of the night, we heard a knock of the door, and we had KGB.
And in Russian they said to us, pack, pack, quick.
And they said-- look, we were completely surprised because my father didn't do anything so wrong.
He didn't because people were dealing.
People were selling, so-- so we thought maybe it's-- maybe it's somebody else that they were thinking.
But look, there was no discussion.
It was KGB.
They said, pack, pack.
We pack, and they took us to the station.
They put us in carriages, in cattle carriages, and we started a journey, which lasted nine weeks, which was a horror.
We didn't know where we go.
We didn't know why.
We completely didn't know why they thought we deserve it.
What happened?
And why that I remember, we couldn't-- later on in life, you know, I just couldn't even remember what were we eating, and I was asking some people that I remembered.
I said, what do you remember?
What were we given?
What I do remember that when the train would stop at the station, you would get hot water, which was called [NON-ENGLISH].
We were really squashed and those.
There were quite a lot of people in one that--
Yes, later on.
At that stage, I still didn't know where the people were from.
I didn't know who the people are because we so didn't have any-- you know, we were only talking to the people that were together with us in that-- in-- in one-- in that cattle whatever you call it.
But later on, we found out.
And it was terrible because we didn't know where we are going.
Like we were crossing-- it's only when we crossed the Ural Mountains, which was weeks and weeks after-- you know, there was no sanitation.
If you needed to the toilet, when the train would stop, you would go [INAUDIBLE] and do it in the fields.
And what we started to do-- we started like to exchange-- or rather if we could-- we learned quick.
Like we would come to a station, we would exchange like a jumper, my mother's jumper or that, for some food.
And this as far as I remember because I couldn't remember if we were given-- I know that [NON-ENGLISH] were given.
And--
With peasants, yes.
With people-- you know, it was a station with people, even if our Russian was nonexistent almost.
But I saw that, so I said people were coming.
This was the typical thing in Russia because, later on, when we-- when it was the same thing, that people-- if they had some-- some people, this was their profession.
So if they had some kind of food, they would go to the station, and they knew that trains will be coming with people from Vladivostok or somewhere else.
And they would-- there will be an exchange of goods, you know, because money was of no importance.
Money was paper that you couldn't do much with it.
It's food that was number one, and for food, you were willing to give away things.

Anyway, we were-- we were so in debt, trying for weeks-- almost nine weeks, it took us, but when we started to have the-- the feeling that we are being sort like taken into the middle of Russia was when we crossed the Ural Mountains because this is when we realized.
And anyway, one day, they dropped us in the middle of nowhere.
Russian guards.
Russian police, yes.
Russian guards.
No, no, no, we were not beaten.
We were just not on the-- not on the trains.
Later on, there were unpleasant incidents.
But-- but we were taken to a spot, which was completely middle of nowhere, really.
The middle of nowhere because it was far away from a city, far away from a township, far away from everything.
Later on, we were told that do-- whatever they were-- the huts belonged to political prisoners, and they-- so they made a meeting.
They said, this is where you're going to be, and this is-- they said it in Russian.
I can tell you Russian.
They said [NON-ENGLISH], which means, you'll get used to it, and if you will not get used to it, you will die, but die-- not in a nice way.
You know, this is a very unpleasant Russian way of dying.
And it was bitterly cold, and two people from every family, he's-- he said the person in charge-- which was a KGB man, which we had a lot of problems later on.
He said every family has to have two people in order to get a food ration.
But--
Who worked.
But since my mother was always sort of person that couldn't work, and she wasn't well, I was the oldest one, so I was told to go to work.
But before even we started work-- and this was for the first time in my life that I have seen-- they put us-- you know, we were very dirty, traveling that many weeks without a wash.
Everybody had lice.
Everybody was dirty, so they put us into Russian barnyard.
I don't know what it-- what-- how'd you say it in English.
It's like a big bath house.
You know But few hundred women undressed.
You know, I was twelve, and it's for-- for the first time, this wasn't a society like now.
I've never seen a naked woman, and for the first time in my life, I suddenly saw a few hundred women, old and young, everybody naked.
They took away your clothes, and they put it-- it was called delousing, you know, and they sterilize it or whatever.
And we had to have a good wash, scrub, and later on, they got us back the clothes, and we were given little huts also. 10 people into one room.
And how did we survive?
You know, my father was-- well, my father was put to work in a forest, cutting trees, and the trees would go with the river.
They would send them with the river to the nearest whatever.
I don't know what they were using them for.
But look, my father was a person that-- from a city, and I don't think he's ever seen in his life that many trees or all that, but he had to do it.
And it was bitterly cold.
I mean, I was given a job in the kitchen at the beginning to peel potatoes, which didn't last long because you had to bring your own knife.
And I remember bringing a knife, and after a few days, the knife was stolen, and I had problems with the knife.
And they let me off, they said, because I was very young, that if I want to go to school, then they'll let me off work, which was really wonderful because I did go to school.
The only thing is that the school-- it was a little school seven kilometers away, and it was bitterly cold.
It was minus 40, and we didn't have enough clothes, but I still-- I was very determined.
I would go to snow in-- I was the only one actual because my brother and sister didn't go-- and in a bitter cold, and I went to school.
I learned Russian.
That was the beginning.
I learned--
There was no other way.
You know, I was the only one that went to school because the younger kids couldn't go to school.
They were too young.
The older kids went to work.
I was sort of like in the middle, and they allowed me to go to school.
I was also very-- I always sought-- tried to learn whatever I could.
And maybe I-- maybe they-- they realized, and they let me go to school.
The only--
Yeah, I'll tell you what, I couldn't run away.
I was 12, and later on 13.
My parents also never thought about driving away.
But many people did run away, but nobody-- that's the interesting part.
Many people run away.
Many people were shot, and many people froze to death.
We were so far away from anything that there was no way that you could drop away.
And a lot of people-- I think that, from a two and a half thousand-- they were saying two and a half thousand that we started, that came-- that when we came, only a few hundred survived.
It was a very rough time, and again, the same situation.
Even the-- the bread that we got, that wasn't hardly enough to live on.
I mean, you couldn't live on that.
So-- so I remember the first winter, we-- my brother and myself would go to where some fields-- that there were some peasants not far away-- and we would just steal potatoes.
You know, we would-- we would-- under the snow, with our bare hands, [INAUDIBLE] would steal potatoes.
And this is how-- and this-- the months of winter, I remember having potatoes, and we're very lucky.
My mother would make potatoes in all different shapes and variation, you know, because this is the only food that one could get there the way we got it, and we were scared to if they would catch us.
I don't know what would have happened.
And so this part was the part where we were, which was called [NON-ENGLISH].
That camp there was a very-- as I said, a few hundred people survived.
Mostly people that had families were better off in a way because, somehow, you combined the bread ration and that.
But there was a lot of single people, and single people were dying out.
Terrible.
I myself-- I became ill.
I got diphtheria after a few months, and they put me-- there was no hospitals, but there was quite, where my school was, there was also-- they called it an [NON-ENGLISH].
This was a room where there was a nurse.
So they put me in that room, and I was there quite a few weeks because this was catchy, and I was scared that-- that I haven't seen-- my mother never came once, even.
She couldn't walk.
My father came a couple of times and got me some chicken soup, and-- because he was working, so from work, he dropped in.
And I was quite a while until I recovered and went back [INAUDIBLE].
Then this was from the time that we came, which this was a very-- when I think about all those years in Russia, this was our worst time from every point of view.
From we were haggard.
We were starving.
From bitterly cold, and a lot of-- there was sickness.
A lot of people died from-- from there, and a lot of people were killed also, the ones that tried to run away and that.
There were a lot of people like us that the Russians were uncomfortable with.

You know, they were thinking-- oh, they knew that we had jewels and that we run away from the Germans, but what we didn't know that they have a plan, a new war, that they want to start a war with Germany, and we were an unwanted element.
They didn't want people on the border that were-- that were Polish citizens.
I was told by some other people that they were made to take Russian passports, or approached to take Russian passports.
I don't remember, not to my best knowledge.
I never remember being-- my parents being approached and taking Russian.
There was nobody there that asked them if they want Russian passports or not.
I just remember the KGB coming and putting us in those cattle trains.
--been In Siberia a year and a half.
But when we heard that we are free-- and later on, I spoke to many people, what does it mean to be free?
We were thousands and thousands kilometers away from a human being.
We had no money.
On one hand, many people stayed on because there was-- it wasn't an easy thing to get out of there.
My father worked it out that it'll be better for us if we move because he didn't think that we can survive another winter.
So what he decided that we will sell whatever we have, clothes and bed linen and that, just to be able to buy some tickets-- it doesn't matter where-- somewhere to south of Russia.
Because south means also warm weather, and warm weather is much easier to survive than in cold weather.
Those were-- anyway, this is how he thought.
Later on, not everything turned out 100%, but we tried to-- to get out of there.
We were able to come to the big city, which was called Sverdlovsk.
Why we were-- why we're freed?
There was this-- this is actual a very important thing.
There was an agreement.
You know, the-- the Polish government in exile-- there was a Polish government in exile in London, and the Polish government in exile made a pact with the Russians.
This was against Germany.
And you know at the beginning, it was the Germans and the Russians, but at that stage, the Polish government in exile made a pact of non-aggression.
And since we were Polish citizens, and we have a government in exile, so they freed us.
I heard later on that not every Polish citizen was freed, but we were freed.
That whole camp was free, and we came to the big city Sverdlovsk, and we started to plan how should we go further, because Sverdlovsk is still in, in Ural Mountains.
It's still very far north, and we didn't want to stay.
We didn't want another winter.
Anyway, we couldn't stay also in a big city.
So somehow-- you know, how-- how did we know all that?
You met hundreds of people.
You met a lot of refugees.

From every camp, people would come, and you were talking, and that-- and everybody somehow said that they are all going south, that south, it's an easier climate, and that south sort thinks there are more factories, more work, you know.
Also, everybody was scared.
You know, Siberia Urals are always so connected with-- somehow for us, it was criminals, political refugees, you know, and we wanted to get away as far as we can.
So we bought some tickets.
For money, that we've sold-- we were almost practical was out anything already because we've sold whatever we had there.
And we bought some tickets, and we boarded. again-- and there were again cattle trains.
Russia, that stage, did not-- I mean, not for normal citizens.
[INAUDIBLE] They were already not as bad as the cattle trains that we were sent to Siberia, but they were also cattle trains.
So what was the main difference between--
The main difference was that we were free.
That there was no guard.
That we could go.
And this is what happened.
That like at every station my farther, one of us, either my brother and or me, we would go down.
We also didn't know where the train will stop.
It would stop whenever it wanted.
And we always hoped that, that so we'll catch it.
We had actual, we had once a terrible thing.
That was [RUSSIAN] that was not far from where we were going.
The train stopped.
My father and my brother jumped off.
They were going to buy some food.
And the train started soon after.
My father had enough time to jump and catch the train, and my brother got lost.
Can you imagine?
I don't know, my brother was 11 years probably at that stage.
My mother was terrible.
We were thinking that we've lost him already.
And we were sure, as a matter of fact, because there were a thousand refugees and nobody-- the trains were irregular.
There was no food.
And it didn't come to mind even that he heard where we were going.
But then when we came to the place where we were talking about, he was standing, waiting for us this.
This is an 11-year-old boy.
And you know, it was a war going on.
He heard.
Look, we were much more aware.
This is the difference.
I very often say to my children that you just can't imagine at the stage when we were 12 or 11, the things that we did.
You just don't-- our kids are completely not aware.
You know look, this was an 11-year-old boy.
He heard my parents speaking.
He heard us talk that this is where we are going.
And he caught-- he came before us.
When we came he was standing at the station awaiting for us.
But it was a week my mother thought that we've lost him.
You know, and it was a very sad week for us.
Right, so now where, where, where did I finish?
Ah, so we were in that cattle in the train.
On the way to Sverdlovsk.
On the way to Sverdlovsk.
And I remember sort-- also look, I don't remember it with-- with-- with love.
I remember that we would at a station run for some food.
Sometimes we had to change that train for another train.
And I remember we would wait for days for another train.
I remember we would put out a coat or a blanket or whatever.
And we would sleep there.
And I remember I wasn't well.
Somehow, my mom told it's rheumatism.
And she said she's never seen a young child.
But apparently, when I was thinking back later on, I thought it's not rheumatism.
I probably had temperature.
I very often had sore throats.
Because later on I also had a lot of problems with my, with my throat.
And I was sick.
The journey wasn't-- so for me, was a very unpleasant one.
But finally we came to that spot after we've changed trains and that.
We came to the spot that my father intended, which was called Merke.
This was in Kazakhstan.
This is the middle of Asia.
And it was-- look it was also, I used to call it the loft.
You can't imagine what kind of accommodation.
You know, like the huts were made of clay.
The floors were clay.
There was completely everything there was dust and clay.
I've seen later on films like it was very close to the Chinese border, almost on the Chinese border.
And the only thing, that my father got a job the way he was thinking.
Because there was a [INAUDIBLE].
This was a big factory, beet root, where they're making sugar out of beet root.
And a lot of people so went for it.
And actually, it saved a lot of lives.
Because my mother would cook a big pot of beet root.
And there were a lot of lonely people, single people.
And she would feed whoever was hungry, really.
And my father got a job.
And somehow life settled.
Why did he intend to go to Merke?
Was there a particular reason?
There was no, really.
You know, many-- this is the very interesting part that everything at that stage was done-- look we never, we didn't know what Merke is.
We didn't know what Kazakhstan is.
We could have-- you know Merke was a spot that somebody told him that there is that sugar factory.
So he worked it out in his mind.
A lot of things were a matter of luck during the war, you know.
A lot of our friends later on, afterward, that we met some went to [INAUDIBLE] and some went-- I met now, I met a lady that was five or six kilometers from close to Merke, but I've never knew her.
She was also there.
People went where they felt that maybe they'll be able to survive there.
You know, a small thing will trigger it off.
Like because there was a big-- that [INAUDIBLE] people we're thinking, well maybe we'll get a job there.
Having a job, you've got already chance to survive.
And that was the reason.
Also he told it's warm, it's a warmer climate.
It was warm, but it wasn't a good climate.
It was exactly like, like north of China.
The winters were bitterly cold, and the summers were very hot.
But the conditions that we were living were also terrible.
And a lot, a lot of people died from typhoid.
A lot.
You know, the whole-- look, one day you get up in the morning, this man died, that woman died.
It was really terrible.
But why I never complained, because more or less, we had a reasonable life.
You know, in Merke I went to school.
And I came to Merke, I almost, my Russian was very poor.
And I finished high school.
I finished matric, which was a very big achievement for somebody that was a refugee.
And in the conditions that we we're living, it's very hard to explain.
We had one room made out of clay.
Everything there was clay.
Everything there was clay.
And like what we did, you couldn't wash the floor there.
But we would go out and find some manure.
You know, there was a cow or a horse.
And we'll take some manure with some water and spread on the floor.
And that kept the dust from coming out.
So the conditions were terrible.
I remember we had a stove made out of clay.
And I used to be very cold.
When everybody would go to bed, I had to study.
So I would sit near that stove, and very often I would break it.
And in the morning they would good up and the stove was broke.
I they were very angry because they would have to make a new stove.
You know, the conditions were really-- you see them in the poorest of the poorest now in China or-- or what.
But for us, it was lucky.
Because it made us survive the war, really.
It was something that we were dreaming of when we were in Siberia.
You know, we were dreaming.
Because in a way, we were free.
In a way, I had friends.
There was not that many Jews.
There were a lot of Russian refugees.
No, I mean, I'm not correct.
There were not many, not a lot of Polish Jews.
There were a lot of Russian Jews that run away, because this was already the war.
And a lot of Russian Jews that the children went to the war, and the elderly couples or little children where so that run away from Germans were also living in that Merke.
And we were very sort-- look, it was for us a very-- the atmosphere for us was very good.
Because there were Russian Jews, as I said.
There was that atmosphere of helping each other.
The conditions were terrible.
Right, OK.
